title: Manifolds With Curvature Bounded Below In the Spectral Sense
speaker: Gioacchino Antonelli
abstract:
In this talk, I will discuss some results concerning the geometry and topology of manifolds on which the first eigenvalue of the operator -γΔ + Ric is bounded below. Here, γ is a positive number, Δ is the Laplacian, and Ric denotes the pointwise lowest eigenvalue of the Ricci tensor. 
First, I will discuss some motivations and applications of such a study. Then, I will discuss spectral generalizations of the Bishop -- Gromov and Bonnet -- Myers theorems. If time allows, I will discuss a spectral generalization of the Cheeger -- Gromoll splitting theorem as well.
This is joint work with M. Pozzetta and K. Xu.

proof_stages.label: Classical comparison-geometry benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Ricci and scalar curvature, comparison with constant-curvature models, and the Bishop-Gromov/Bonnet-Myers theorem giving sharp diameter, volume, and finite-fundamental-group conclusions from the pointwise lower bound Ricci >= n-1.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for spectral curvature from scalar curvature and stable hypersurfaces
proof_stages.summary: Naive scalar-curvature analogues fail, but scalar or bi-Ricci curvature lower bounds on an ambient manifold induce spectral lower bounds on stable minimal hypersurfaces, motivating spectral curvature as a global weakening of pointwise curvature.

proof_stages.label: Definition and elementary behavior of spectral Ricci lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: The Ricci tensor is replaced by its lowest-eigenvalue function rho, and the condition is that the Schrödinger operator -gamma Delta + rho have first eigenvalue at least lambda; pointwise Ricci bounds imply it, while spectral gaps show the converse can fail.

proof_stages.label: Sharp spectral Bishop-Gromov theorem
proof_stages.summary: For compact n-manifolds, n >= 3, satisfying the sharp spectral lower bound with gamma = (n-1)/(n-2), the volume is at most that of the unit sphere and the fundamental group is finite, with rigidity at equality and no comparable control above the sharp gamma threshold.

proof_stages.label: Weighted isoperimetric-profile proof of the volume bound
proof_stages.summary: Adapting Bray’s proof of Bishop-Gromov, the speaker replaces the ordinary isoperimetric profile by a weighted profile built from a positive first eigenfunction of -gamma Delta + rho, obtains the same sharp differential concavity inequality as on the sphere, and compares extinction times to bound volume and then pi_1.

proof_stages.label: Diameter bounds with dependence on the spectral ground state
proof_stages.summary: Although the sharp volume theorem does not yield a uniform diameter bound in general, a Bonnet-Myers-type estimate holds when a positive subsolution has controlled sup/inf ratio, and it can be proved either along weighted geodesics or via a prescribed-mean-curvature barrier argument.

proof_stages.label: Discussion of possible intermediate-dimensional weighted controls
proof_stages.summary: In response to a question, the speaker explains that analogues between weighted length control and weighted volume control for intermediate-dimensional minimizing objects would require identifying the curvature operator appearing in their second variation.

examples.name: The cylinder R x S^2 with positive scalar curvature
examples.note: The product cylinder is used to show that a uniform positive scalar-curvature lower bound alone does not force compactness, diameter bounds, or volume bounds, motivating the shift toward stable hypersurfaces and spectral curvature conditions.
examples.engagement_reason: the cylinder is used as a quick counterexample to scalar-curvature analogues, with only stated facts such as positive scalar curvature and unbounded size

passes.reason: Used as an ordinary noncompact positive-scalar-curvature product showing scalar lower bounds alone do not force compactness, volume, or diameter control.

examples.name: Hyperbolic space as a spectrally positive Ricci example for large gamma
examples.note: The spectral gap of H^n shows that adding enough Laplacian term can make negative pointwise Ricci curvature satisfy a positive spectral Ricci condition, explaining why compactness or sharp gamma thresholds are necessary.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the spectral gap and Ricci eigenvalue for hyperbolic space to derive the gamma threshold for spectral Ricci positivity

passes.reason: Hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric constant-negative-curvature model and is used as a noncompact spectrally Ricci-positive enemy for large gamma.
Hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric constant-negative-curvature model and is used as a noncompact enemy to spectral Ricci lower-bound analogues for large gamma.

Hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric constant negative curvature model and is used as a noncompact enemy showing spectrally positive Ricci curvature for large gamma need not imply compactness or pointwise Ricci positivity.
Hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric constant-negative-curvature model and is used as a noncompact enemy to spectral Ricci positivity for large gamma.

Hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric constant-negative-curvature model with the sharp spectral-gap threshold, used as a noncompact enemy to spectral Ricci compactness statements for large gamma.

Hyperbolic space becomes spectrally Ricci-positive for large gamma and is presented as the noncompact enemy to naive Bonnet--Myers generalization; it is a maximally symmetric constant-curvature model.

Used as the noncompact enemy showing spectral Ricci positivity for large gamma need not imply pointwise positivity or compactness; hyperbolic space is maximally symmetric with the sharp spectral-gap threshold.
Used as the noncompact ‘enemy’ showing spectral Ricci positivity for large gamma need not imply compactness; hyperbolic space is maximally symmetric and sits at the sharp spectral-gap threshold.

used as a noncompact obstruction to Bonnet--Myers-type conclusions for spectrally positive Ricci when gamma is large; hyperbolic space is the maximally symmetric negative space form with the sharp spectral-gap threshold.

examples.name: Compact quotients of hyperbolic space
examples.note: An audience question about hyperbolic quotients clarifies that compact hyperbolic manifolds with large volume or infinite fundamental group cannot satisfy the theorem’s spectral assumption at the stated sharp constant.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker and audience reason through whether compact hyperbolic quotients could satisfy the spectral condition, using their volume/fundamental group and changed Laplacian eigenvalue to rule it out

passes.reason: Compact hyperbolic quotients are locally symmetric constant-curvature manifolds raised as a possible obstruction but then excluded from the theorem’s spectral hypothesis.

Compact hyperbolic quotients are locally symmetric space forms raised as large-volume/infinite-fundamental-group adversarial cases, ultimately showing they cannot satisfy the stated spectral hypothesis.

Compact hyperbolic quotients are locally symmetric space forms, but in the discussion they are only a proposed obstruction and are rejected since compactness gives a zero Laplacian eigenvalue so the stated positive spectral Ricci condition cannot hold.
